checks.a_analysis: The output text strictly follows the content of the source PDF. No invented headings, introductions, or body text were found. The headings ('NC Parcels', 'NC Parcels Project Status by County', 'Thank you!') and paragraph text ('October 7, 2025', contact info) correspond exactly to the source.

Every email address in the output ('katie.doherty@rutherfordcountync.gov', 'relkins@sosnc.gov', 'elizabeth.daniel@nc.gov') was checked against page 3 of the source PDF. They are identical. No other contact information (phone numbers, URLs) is present in the source or output.

The source PDF has 3 pages. The JSON output contains 3 sections, each corresponding to one page of the PDF. Section 1 covers page 1, Section 2 covers page 2, and Section 3 covers page 3. No pages or major sections are missing.
There are no tables in the source document. The status information on page 2 is presented as a legend for a map, not a structured table. Consequently, this check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All text content from the source is accounted for in the output. While the legend and source information on page 2 are not present in separate 'paragraph' elements, the full information is captured in the AI-generated alt-text for the map image in that section. Per the audit instructions, image alt-text is expected to be AI-generated and the primary goal is ensuring the output contains the same information as the source.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON paragraphs and headings matches the source exactly. Formatting (bold, italics) is appropriately applied to match the visual style of the source (e.g., italics for the date on page 1, bold for 'Contacts:' on page 3). No changes in meaning were detected.

checks.a_analysis: The date 'October 7, 2025' appears in both Section 1 and Section 2. This accurately reflects the source document, where the date is present on both the title slide (Page 1) and the map slide (Page 2). No other instances of text duplication were found.

The document utilizes section titles ('NC Parcels', 'NC Parcels Project Status by County', and 'Thank you!') as primary headings. These titles correspond to the main text elements on each page of the source. The hierarchy is logical and follows the document's flow without skipping levels.

There are no tables in the source document or the converted output. The map legend on page 2 is not a table, and its content has been incorporated into the image alt text as permitted for graphical data.

The reading order in the output (Section 1, 2, 3) perfectly mirrors the page order of the source PDF. Within each section, the elements (Title -> Date -> Image/Links) follow the logical top-to-bottom layout of the slides.
The source document does not contain any lists. The contact information on page 3 is correctly rendered as a single paragraph containing text and links.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The HTML metadata indicates 0 headings in the semantic.json but 3 in the rendered HTML. This discrepancy is due to the JSON schema using 'sections[].title' fields rather than internal 'type: heading' elements. The renderer correctly identifies these section titles as headings, resulting in 3 headings in the final HTML. As the structural headings are present and correctly rendered, this is considered a successful conversion.

The three email addresses found on page 3 of the source document are correctly captured as links in Section 3 of the output. The mismatch of one link in the HTML metadata (3 in JSON vs 4 in HTML) is typically due to an automatically added footer or metadata link, which is an expected pipeline addition.

checks.a_analysis: The visual organization of the document is faithfully maintained in the HTML conversion. The single-column flow follows the page order of the source PDF: title page content, followed by the map and project status, ending with the closing and contact information. While the background colors and specific alignments differ (which is expected in HTML conversion), the overall information hierarchy and sequence are preserved.
There are no traditional data tables present in the source PDF. The map legend on page 2 is rendered correctly as part of the map graphic.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present in the HTML output. This includes the large NCDIT logo from page 1, the entire map graphic from page 2 (which includes the NCGICC logo, north arrow, and scale), and the NCDIT logo from the first page is reused. A small redundant NCDIT logo from the bottom of page 3 is omitted in the HTML rendering, which is acceptable as it is a repeated branding element already established at the start of the document.

There is a visual duplication of text content associated with the map section. The heading 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' and the date 'October 7, 2025' are rendered as HTML text at the top of the map section, but they are also visibly included inside the image graphic that immediately follows. This causes the same heading and date to appear twice in immediate succession.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 2 of the PDF contains the title 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' and date 'October 7, 2025' within the map's layout frame.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' and 'October 7, 2025' as text headings, followed by an image containing the same exact text strings within its top border.
checks.b_evidence_location: Middle of HTML output, corresponding to Page 2 of the PDF.

checks.a_analysis: The overall visual quality of the HTML conversion is high. The text is clear, images are sharp and correctly sized, and the layout is clean with appropriate whitespace. The contact links are correctly converted and styled with icons.
